fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This low-fee (0.20%) index fund gives you instant exposure to India's 150 mid-sized companies. It's like buying a basket of growing businesses automatically. Great core holding for your aggressive 20-year plan.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund- Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: A smaller set of 250 companies that can grow faster than big firms. This fund has historically returned 17% over 5 years. Like Medium Cap, it's equity-taxed (12.5% tax on gains after 1 year) and low-fee.

fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Big, stable companies you can see on any news channel (TCS, HDFC, Reliance). This fund has returned 17.7% over 5 years. Even aggressive plans need some ballast here.

fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: An all-active fund from Bandhan with 14 years of track record. It's picked small companies on its own. Over 5 years it returned 23.7% — higher than the index alternative above. Prefer this fund for lumpsum since you have cash waiting.

fund.name: quant Small Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: A very active quant fund that uses data to pick small stocks. Over 5 years returned 23.2%. We give it SIP-only entry because its past returns are exceptional but that doesn't guarantee future results — spread your risk.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Another active small-cap fund from Nippon (a better-known house). Over 5 years, 22.3% returns. Good diversification so you're not too dependent on one fund manager.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a Direct Growth mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, Coin, or MFU (any one is fine). During KYC, ensure your PAN, Aadhaar, and bank account are linked to get direct plans (lower yearly fees).

Step 2: Make a one-time lumpsum of ₹1,000,000 split as follows into Direct Growth plans: ₹800,000 to Motilal Oswal Midcap (code 147622), ₹400,000 to Bandhan Small Cap (code 147946). These will be your biggest one-time placements when you return from sea.

Step 3: Start monthly SIPs of ₹24,000 in Code 147622, ₹12,000 in Code 147623, ₹9,000 in Code 118632, ₹9,000 in Code 147946, ₹6,000 in Code 120828, and ₹4,800 in Code 118778. Choose the 'Growth Option' (not Dividend) so growth compounds automatically.

Step 4: Use third-party apps like Zerodha Coin, Kuvera, or Groww directly — they show up-to-date NAVs and let you track performance easily.
review_checkpoints:
After 6 months: confirm all SIPs are running on time and no money got stuck in regular (bad) plan instead of Direct.

After 1 year: check if any of your 6 funds have underperformed their respective index by 5% or more. If yes, compare with peers in the same category and consider switching that fund to another top performer from the curated list.

After 3 years, 5 years, and 10 years: do a full rebalance — see if your actual mix has strayed more than 15% from the target 40/20/15/15/10/8 split, and adjust by selling or buying.

Rational for this plan: Captain H, at 40 with 20 years ahead and no liabilities, your goals (₹8Cr retirement, land/house abroad, daughter's studies) slot into a high-equity mix perfectly. You earn well at sea, so the ability to lumpsum when ashore is a huge advantage — we use it. Our mix leans heavily into the mid/small caps you asked for, but with a solid index core (40% midcap 150, plus 20% smallcap index) that keeps costs low (all funds have expense ratios under 1.3%). That's the secret sauce — low fees + active small caps = better long-term wealth.

Risks: Your portfolio is 85% equity. Stock markets can drop 20–30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time (often in 2–3 years). Small-cap funds have seen 30%+ corrections before. That's fine: you have 20 years ahead, and history shows big moves on both sides. Just keep SIPs running and don't panic-sell.

Projected returns: base CAGR ~16.5% (weighted avg: 40%×17% + 20%×15% + 15%×15% + 15%×24% + 10%×23% + 8%×22%), bull CAGR ~20.5% (base +4%), bear CAGR ~12.5% (base -4%). These are estimates, not guarantees.
Disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check once a year (or after big market moves). If any holding has grown to >55% of your total equity allocation, trim a bit and buy laggards to get back to the 40/20/15/15/10/8 target. For a sea-faring life, you can wait until the next shore leave to execute the trades.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time.
Small-cap funds have higher volatility — they can fall 30%+ in a correction, but also surge 50%+ in a recovery.
Lumpsum investing when markets are high can mean short-term losses before recovery. That's why we stagger entry with SIPs.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning: The plan allocates 60% to Index funds (passive) and 40% to Active Funds (Large & Small Cap), yielding a passive_instrument_fraction of 0.6. The turnover_score is 1.0 (highy passive) because rebalancing is strictly annual or event-driven after 55% drift, with no tactical/swing trading mentioned. The cost_emphasis_score is 0.7 as the rationale explicitly mentions 'low fees' and using 'Direct Plans' as a strategic choice to minimize costs. The research_vs_cost_score is 0.4 (moderate) because the plan references past performance of active managers to justify over-index funds, but largely relies on index-based exposure for the core; it lacks deep fundamental analysis of individual stocks. The time_horizon_alignment_score is 1.0 (highly patient) given the 20-year horizon, buy-and-hold SIP approach, and explicit instruction to 'not panic-sell' during corrections. The portfolio_activeness_score is 0.53: the 60% passive core is truly passive (0.0 component), but the 40% active sleeve (Large Cap 15%, Small Caps 25%) consists of active funds; with no specific risk metrics (beta/alpha) provided in the input, the score is an inference based on the categorical mix, resulting in a portfolio weighted average reflecting moderate active tactics.

pqs.reasoning: The plan has a 0.9 Goal Alignment because the aggressive 85% equity mix perfectly matches the investor's profile (40yo, aggressive risk, 20yr horizon), and the preference for index+small-cap satellite is honored. Internal Consistency is high (0.95) as the rationale, allocations, and risk warnings tell a coherent story. Tax Efficiency is strong (0.9) because all funds are equity-oriented (>65% equity), ensuring favorable LTCG taxation for the 'new_regime' investor, despite no need for 80C filling. Risk-Return Appropriateness is good (0.85) with realistic projections and acknowledgment of volatility, though it lacks specific downside hedging strategies. Diversification is the weak point (0.4). The plan suffers from severe 'excess diversification' within a single risky asset class: it holds THREE different active Small Cap funds (Bandhan, Quant, Nippon) in addition to a Small Cap Index fund. This creates unnecessary manager selection beta, overlap, and fee inefficiency compared to a simpler 2-3 fund portfolio. It violates the principle that diversification should span asset classes and geographies, not just add multiple funds to one volatile bucket.
